front: Ruler: nicknamed "Longshanks" for his long legs and the "Hammer of the Scots" (Malleus Scotorum) after his wars against Scotland
Ruler: succeeded his father, Henry III, as King of England
Event: once threw his youngest daughter Elizabeth's golden coronet into a fire during a fit of anger while planning her wedding to John I, Count of Holland
Event: this campaign was completed after Llywelyn ap Gruffudd ("Llywelyn the Last") was killed at the Battle of Orewin Bridge in 1282
Event: this campaign was provoked in part when Llywelyn ap Gruffudd married Eleanor de Montfort
Event: Edward I employed the Savoyard architect James of Saint George to build an "Iron Ring" of castles around North Wales, including Beaumaris, Conwy, Caernarfon, and Harlech
Law: this 1284 statute formally annexed Wales, imposing English-speaking sheriffs and common law on new counties
Event: Edward I first granted this title, held by the heir to the English throne, after completing the conquest of Wales
Event: Edward I financed the conquest of Wales by granting the Riccardi of Lucca control of customs collections on wool exports
Event: Archbishop John Peckham tried to negotiate an end to this campaign, but the Welsh side denounced Edward as worse than Saracens in the "Declaration of Defiance"
Event: according to legend, completion of this campaign inspired the "last bard" to issue a curse before jumping off a cliff
Treaty: this 1277 agreement, which subdued Wales, was later replaced by the Treaty of Aberconwy
War: Edward I personally led English forces to victory over William Wallace at this 1298 battle
Event: Edward I judged this succession dispute over the vacant Scottish throne, involving claimants including John Balliol and John Hastings
Event: Edward I installed John Balliol as a puppet Scottish king after the Great Cause, then later imprisoned him
War: Edward I won this 1296 battle after the Treaty of Birgham, which had arranged his son's marriage to Margaret, Maid of Norway, collapsed with her death
Event: Edward I seized this stone, on which Scottish kings were traditionally inaugurated, and incorporated it into England's Coronation Chair
War: Edward I's forces under Hugh de Cressingham were routed at this 1297 battle, where William Wallace and Andrew de Moray exploited a bottleneck
Event: Edward I later captured and executed this Scottish rebel leader
Treaty: this original 1295 mutual-defense pact between John Balliol and Philip IV of France was formed specifically to oppose Edward I
Event: Edward I constructed what is believed to be the largest trebuchet ever built, nicknamed this, during the siege of Stirling Castle
Event: in 1290, Edward I ordered the expulsion of England's entire Jewish population, a religious minority forced to leave partly for practicing usury
back: Expulsion of the Jews from England (1290)
work: Expulsion of the Jews from England (1290)

front: Event: this expulsion was tied to Edward I's own money problems and preceded by the hanging of hundreds of people accused of "coin-clipping"
back: Expulsion of the Jews from England (1290)
work: Expulsion of the Jews from England (1290)

front: Event: this expulsion was partly inspired by blood libel myths surrounding the deaths of William of Norwich and Little Saint Hugh of Lincoln
back: Expulsion of the Jews from England (1290)
work: Expulsion of the Jews from England (1290)

front: Event: before this expulsion, officials were ordered to seal up the "archae," community chests used to store Jewish moneylenders' bonds
back: Expulsion of the Jews from England (1290)
work: Expulsion of the Jews from England (1290)

front: Event: during this expulsion, one ship captain named Henry Adrian abandoned Jewish passengers on a sandbar to drown at high tide
back: Expulsion of the Jews from England (1290)
work: Expulsion of the Jews from England (1290)

front: Event: Archbishop John le Romeyne threatened excommunication against anyone who harassed the departing participants of this expulsion
back: Expulsion of the Jews from England (1290)
work: Expulsion of the Jews from England (1290)

front: Event: the Domus Conversorum ("House of Converts") was eventually repurposed as a home for clerks after this expulsion
back: Expulsion of the Jews from England (1290)
work: Expulsion of the Jews from England (1290)

front: Event: this 1290 expulsion was not reversed until Oliver Cromwell's era, when he sought to resettle wealthy Jewish traders from Amsterdam in London
back: Expulsion of the Jews from England (1290)
work: Expulsion of the Jews from England (1290)

front: Institution: this 1295 Parliament for the first time drew in representatives of the lesser clergy alongside elected representatives of the shires and boroughs
Law: this 1290 statute, also called the Third Statute of Westminster, banned subinfeudation and let tenants sell land freely, hastening the end of feudalism
Law: these 1279/1290 statutes banned donating land to the Church without royal license, protecting royal revenue
Event: Edward I ordered this 1279 census to investigate corruption among local officials, shortly after returning from the Ninth Crusade
Event: Archbishop Robert Winchelsey's publication of the papal bull Clericis Laicos blocked Edward I's attempt to collect half of clerical revenues
Law: Edward I imposed this unpopular wool export tax
Figure: this regent of Edward I used quo warranto proceedings to claim disputed royal lands back from the nobility
Event: after Eleanor of Castile's death in 1290, Edward I erected twelve stone crosses along the route of her funeral procession from Harby to Westminster Abbey
work: Eleanor of Castile & the Eleanor Crosses

front: War: as a young prince, Edward escaped captivity to lead royalist forces to victory over Simon de Montfort at this 1265 battle, ending the Second Barons' War
War: as a young prince fighting alongside Gilbert de Clare, Edward was captured by baronial forces at this 1264 battle before escaping
Event: on the Ninth Crusade, Edward survived a poison-dagger assassination attempt by a member of the Order of Assassins after an unsuccessful raid on this Levantine city
Event: a discredited legend claims Eleanor of Castile sucked the poison from Edward's wound after his Ninth Crusade assassination attempt
Figure: this seneschal of Edward I was killed while leading a surprise attack from a pontoon bridge during the Ninth Crusade
Event: Edward cut short his participation in the Ninth Crusade to return home upon learning of his father Henry III's death, becoming king in absentia
Play: George Peele's Elizabethan history play about this king shows his defeats of Llywelyn ap Gruffudd and John Balliol and ends with Queen Elinor's confession that she did not father Joan
back: The Famous Chronicle of King Edward the First (George Peele)
